notes.note: The letter-heading. The recipient is P. Sulpicius Rufus, then holding the title imperator as proconsul of Illyricum (48-46 BC); he is a different man from the famous orator and tribune of 88 BC of the same name. "S." abbreviates salutem (dicit), "sends greetings."

notes.anchor: I voted gladly for a public thanksgiving on your behalf
notes.note: A supplicatio: a formal period of public thanksgiving to the gods decreed by the Senate to honour a victorious commander, and a normal precursor to a triumph. Cicero's vote here is an act of senatorial courtesy marking Sulpicius's military success in Illyricum.

notes.anchor: Dionysius, my slave, who handled my library
notes.note: A literate slave entrusted with a wealthy Roman's library would catalogue, copy, and maintain the book-rolls; such men were valuable and the loss of one with his master's books was a real grievance. This Dionysius is not to be confused with Cicero's freedman scholar M. Pomponius Dionysius.

notes.anchor: when he said he had been manumitted by me, they believed him
notes.note: Manumission (manu missio) freed a slave; a freedman could move about and do business openly. By falsely claiming Cicero had freed him, the runaway Dionysius passed as a free man and so escaped recapture at Narona.
